question: What is the definition of 'cross-border processing' according to Article 4?
gold_answer: Either processing of personal data which takes place in the context of the activities of establishments in more than one Member State of a controller or processor in the Union where the controller or processor is established in more than one Member State; or processing of personal data which takes place in the context of the activities of a single establishment of a controller or processor in the Union but which substantially affects or is likely to substantially affect data subjects in more than one Member State.

question: Under what circumstances is processing of personal data revealing racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious or philosophical beliefs, or trade union membership allowed?
gold_answer: Processing of personal data revealing racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious or philosophical beliefs, or trade union membership is allowed if the data subject has given explicit consent to the processing of those personal data for one or more specified purposes, except where Union or Member State law provide that the prohibition referred to in paragraph 1 may not be lifted by the data subject; processing is necessary for the purposes of carrying out the obligations and exercising specific rights of the controller or of the data subject in the field of employment and social security and social protection law in so far as it is authorised by Union or Member State law or a collective agreement pursuant to Member State law providing for appropriate safeguards for the fundamental rights and the interests of the data subject; processing is necessary to protect the vital interests of the data subject or of another natural person where the data subject is physically or legally incapable of giving consent; processing is carried out in the course of its legitimate activities with appropriate safeguards by a foundation, association or any other not-for-profit body with a political, philosophical, religious or trade union aim and on condition that the processing relates solely to the members or to former members of the body or to persons who have regular contact with it in connection with its purposes and that the personal data are not disclosed outside that body without the consent of the data subjects; processing relates to personal data which are manifestly made public by the data subject; processing is necessary for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims or whenever courts are acting in their judicial capacity; processing is necessary for reasons of substantial public interest, on the basis of Union or Member State law which shall be proportionate to the aim pursued, respect the essence of the right to data protection and provide for suitable and specific measures to safeguard the fundamental rights and the interests of the data subject; processing is necessary for reasons of public interest in the area of public health, such as protecting against serious cross-border threats to health or ensuring high standards of quality and safety of health care and of medicinal products or medical devices, on the basis of Union or Member State law which provides for suitable and specific measures to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject, in particular professional secrecy; processing is necessary for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89(1) based on Union or Member State law which shall be proportionate to the aim pursued, respect the essence of the right to data protection and provide for suitable and specific measures to safeguard the fundamental rights and the interests of the data subject.

gold_answer: If the purposes for which a controller processes personal data do not or do no longer require the identification of a data subject by the controller, the controller shall not be obliged to maintain, acquire or process additional information in order to identify the data subject for the sole purpose of complying with this Regulation.

gold_answer: The controller shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures, reviewed and updated where necessary, taking into account the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risks of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons. question: What are the four technical and organisational measures that the controller and processor should implement to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk?
gold_answer: The four technical and organisational measures that the controller and processor should implement to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk are: (a) the pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data; (b) the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems and services; (c) the ability to restore the availability and access to personal data in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident; (d) a process for regularly testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the processing.

question: Under what conditions can a controller or processor transfer personal data to a third country or international organization?
gold_answer: A controller or processor may transfer personal data to a third country or an international organisation only if the controller or processor has provided appropriate safeguards, and on condition that enforceable data subject rights and effective legal remedies for data subjects are available.

3. Derogations: In the absence of both adequacy decisions and appropriate safeguards, transfers may occur under specific conditions (Article 49(1)), such as necessity for: protecting vital interests of the subject or others (1f), concluding/performing a contract (1c), important public interest (1d), or legal claims (1e).', 'full_answer': 'The transfer of personal data to a third country or international organization is governed by a hierarchical set of conditions to ensure the level of protection of natural persons is not undermined (Article 44). 

First, transfers may take place if the Commission has issued an adequacy decision, meaning the third country or organization ensures an adequate level of protection, requiring no specific authorization (Article 45(1)). 

Second, if no adequacy decision exists, a controller or processor may transfer data provided they have implemented appropriate safeguards and that enforceable data subject rights and effective legal remedies are available (Article 46(1)). Examples of such safeguards include contractual clauses (Article 46(3a)).

question: Under what conditions can a transfer or set of transfers of personal data to a third country or an international organisation take place in the absence of an adequacy decision or appropriate safeguards?
gold_answer: A transfer or set of transfers of personal data to a third country or an international organisation shall take place only on one of the following conditions: (a) the data subject has explicitly consented to the proposed transfer, after having been informed of the possible risks of such transfers for the data subject due to the absence of an adequacy decision and appropriate safeguards; (b) the transfer is necessary for the performance of a contract between the data subject and the controller or the implementation of pre-contractual measures taken at the data subject's request; (c) the transfer is necessary for the conclusion or performance of a contract concluded in the interest of the data subject between the controller and another natural or legal person; (d) the transfer is necessary for important reasons of public interest; (e) the transfer is necessary for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims; (f) the transfer is necessary in order to protect the vital interests of the data subject or of other persons, where the data subject is physically or legally incapable of giving consent; (g) the transfer is made from a register which according to Union or Member State law is intended to provide information to the public and which is open to consultation either by the public in general or by any person who can demonstrate a legitimate interest, but only to the extent that the conditions laid down by Union or Member State law for consultation are fulfilled in the particular case.

question: What is the requirement for the member or members of each supervisory authority in performing their tasks and exercising their powers?
gold_answer: The member or members of each supervisory authority shall, in the performance of their tasks and exercise of their powers in accordance with this Regulation, remain free from external influence, whether direct or indirect, and shall neither seek nor take instructions from anybody.

question: What are the tasks of each supervisory authority according to Article 57 of the GDPR Regulation?
gold_answer: Each supervisory authority shall monitor and enforce the application of the GDPR Regulation, promote public awareness and understanding of the risks, rules, safeguards, and rights in relation to processing, advise the national parliament, the government, and other institutions and bodies on legislative and administrative measures relating to the protection of natural persons' rights and freedoms with regard to processing, promote the awareness of controllers and processors of their obligations under this Regulation, provide information to any data subject concerning the exercise of their rights under this Regulation and, if appropriate, cooperate with the supervisory authorities in other Member States to that end, handle complaints lodged by a data subject, or by a body, organization, or association in accordance with Article 80, and investigate, to the extent appropriate, the subject matter of the complaint and inform the complainant of the progress and the outcome of the investigation within a reasonable period, in particular if further investigation or coordination with another supervisory authority is necessary, cooperate with, including sharing information and provide mutual assistance to, other supervisory authorities with a view to ensuring the consistency of application and enforcement of this Regulation, conduct investigations on the application of this Regulation, including on the basis of information received from another supervisory authority or other public authority, monitor relevant developments, insofar as they have an impact on the protection of personal data, in particular the development of information and communication technologies and commercial practices, adopt standard contractual clauses referred to in Article 28(8) and in point (d) of Article 46(2), establish and maintain a list in relation to the requirement for data protection impact assessment pursuant to Article 35(4), give advice on the processing operations referred to in Article 36(2), encourage the drawing up of codes of conduct pursuant to Article 40(1) and provide an opinion and approve such codes of conduct which provide sufficient safeguards, pursuant to Article 40(5), encourage the establishment of data protection certification mechanisms and of data protection seals and marks pursuant to Article 42(1), and approve the criteria of certification pursuant to Article 42(5), where applicable, carry out a periodic review of certifications issued in accordance with Article 42(7), draft and publish the criteria for accreditation of a body for monitoring codes of conduct pursuant to Article 41 and of a certification body pursuant to Article 43, conduct the accreditation of a body for monitoring codes of conduct pursuant to Article 41 and of a certification body pursuant to Article 43, authorize contractual clauses and provisions referred to in Article 46(3), approve binding corporate rules pursuant to Article 47, contribute to the activities of the Board, keep internal records of infringements of this Regulation and of measures taken in accordance with Article 58(2), and fulfill any other tasks related to the protection of personal data.

question: Under what conditions may churches and religious associations continue to apply their existing data protection rules?
gold_answer: Churches and religious associations may continue to apply their existing data protection rules if they are comprehensive and brought into line with the GDPR Regulation.
llm_response: {'answer': 'Churches and religious associations or communities in a Member State may continue to apply their existing comprehensive rules relating to the protection of natural persons with regard to processing, provided that such rules are brought into line with this Regulation (chapter IX, article 91, point 1). question: What is the definition of 'cross-border processing' according to Article 4?
gold_answer: Either processing of personal data which takes place in the context of the activities of establishments in more than one Member State of a controller or processor in the Union where the controller or processor is established in more than one Member State; or processing of personal data which takes place in the context of the activities of a single establishment of a controller or processor in the Union but which substantially affects or is likely to substantially affect data subjects in more than one Member State.

question: What should the request for consent be presented as, if the data subject's consent is given in the context of a written declaration which also concerns other matters?
gold_answer: The request for consent shall be presented in a manner which is clearly distinguishable from the other matters, in an intelligible and easily accessible form, using clear and plain language.
llm_response: {'answer': "When a data subject's consent is provided within a written declaration that also addresses other matters, the request for consent shall be presented in a manner that is clearly distinguishable from those other matters. Furthermore, it must be provided in an intelligible and easily accessible form, utilizing clear and plain language. Any portion of such a declaration that infringes upon this Regulation shall not be binding (chapter II, article 7, point 2).", 'full_answer': 'According to chapter II, article 7, point 2, there are specific formal requirements for requesting consent when it is embedded in a written document containing other subjects. The request for consent must meet three primary criteria: it shall be clearly distinguishable from other matters, it shall be in an intelligible and easily accessible form, and it shall use clear and plain language. The legal consequence of failing to meet these standards is that any part of the declaration constituting an infringement of the Regulation shall not be binding.', 'key_snippet': 'the request for consent shall be presented in a manner which is clearly distinguishable from the other matters', 'chunks': ['chapter II, article 7, point 2'], 'percentage': ['100']}

question: What information must the controller provide to the data subject at the time when personal data are obtained, according to Article 13?
gold_answer: The controller must provide the data subject with the identity and contact details of the controller and, where applicable, of the controller's representative; the contact details of the data protection officer, where applicable; the purposes of the processing for which the personal data are intended as well as the legal basis for the processing; where the processing is based on point (f) of Article 6(1), the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party; the recipients or categories of recipients of the personal data, if any; where applicable, the fact that the controller intends to transfer personal data to a third country or international organisation and the existence or absence of an adequacy decision by the Commission, or in the case of transfers referred to in Article 46 or 47, or the second subparagraph of Article 49(1), reference to the appropriate or suitable safeguards and the means by which to obtain a copy of them or where they have been made available. In addition, the controller must provide the data subject with the period for which the personal data will be stored, or if that is not possible, the criteria used to determine that period; the existence of the right to request from the controller access to and rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing concerning the data subject or to object to processing as well as the right to data portability; where the processing is based on point (a) of Article 6(1) or point (a) of Article 9(2), the existence of the right to withdraw consent at any time, without affecting the lawfulness of processing based on consent before its withdrawal; the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority; whether the provision of personal data is a statutory or contractual requirement, or a requirement necessary to enter into a contract, as well as whether the data subject is obliged to provide the personal data and of the possible consequences of failure to provide such data; the existence of automated decision-making, including profiling, referred to in Article 22(1) and (4) and, at least in those cases, meaningful information about the logic involved, as well as the significance and the envisaged consequences of such processing for the data subject.

Additionally, the controller must provide further information for fair and transparent processing: storage period or criteria; right to withdraw consent; rights to access, rectification, erasure, restriction, objection, and data portability; right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority; and information on automated decision-making, including profiling.', 'full_answer': "Under Article 13, the controller's obligations to the data subject at the time of data collection are divided into primary information and further information necessary for fair and transparent processing.

question: When is a controller or processor not established in the Union exempt from designating a representative in the Union under Article 27?
gold_answer: The obligation laid down in paragraph 1 of this Article shall not apply to: (a) processing which is occasional, does not include, on a large scale, processing of special categories of data as referred to in Article 9(1) or processing of personal data relating to criminal convictions and offences referred to in Article 10, and is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons, taking into account the nature, context, scope and purposes of the processing; or (b) a public authority or body.

However, Article 27, point 2 establishes specific exemptions to this obligation. First, under point 2a, the exemption applies to processing that meets three cumulative criteria: it must be occasional, it must not involve the large-scale processing of special categories of data as defined in Article 9(1) or data regarding criminal convictions and offences as defined in Article 10, and it must be unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons, taking into account the nature, context, scope, and purposes of the processing. question: What are the tasks of each supervisory authority according to Article 57 of the GDPR Regulation?
gold_answer: Each supervisory authority shall monitor and enforce the application of the GDPR Regulation, promote public awareness and understanding of the risks, rules, safeguards, and rights in relation to processing, advise the national parliament, the government, and other institutions and bodies on legislative and administrative measures relating to the protection of natural persons' rights and freedoms with regard to processing, promote the awareness of controllers and processors of their obligations under this Regulation, provide information to any data subject concerning the exercise of their rights under this Regulation and, if appropriate, cooperate with the supervisory authorities in other Member States to that end, handle complaints lodged by a data subject, or by a body, organization, or association in accordance with Article 80, and investigate, to the extent appropriate, the subject matter of the complaint and inform the complainant of the progress and the outcome of the investigation within a reasonable period, in particular if further investigation or coordination with another supervisory authority is necessary, cooperate with, including sharing information and provide mutual assistance to, other supervisory authorities with a view to ensuring the consistency of application and enforcement of this Regulation, conduct investigations on the application of this Regulation, including on the basis of information received from another supervisory authority or other public authority, monitor relevant developments, insofar as they have an impact on the protection of personal data, in particular the development of information and communication technologies and commercial practices, adopt standard contractual clauses referred to in Article 28(8) and in point (d) of Article 46(2), establish and maintain a list in relation to the requirement for data protection impact assessment pursuant to Article 35(4), give advice on the processing operations referred to in Article 36(2), encourage the drawing up of codes of conduct pursuant to Article 40(1) and provide an opinion and approve such codes of conduct which provide sufficient safeguards, pursuant to Article 40(5), encourage the establishment of data protection certification mechanisms and of data protection seals and marks pursuant to Article 42(1), and approve the criteria of certification pursuant to Article 42(5), where applicable, carry out a periodic review of certifications issued in accordance with Article 42(7), draft and publish the criteria for accreditation of a body for monitoring codes of conduct pursuant to Article 41 and of a certification body pursuant to Article 43, conduct the accreditation of a body for monitoring codes of conduct pursuant to Article 41 and of a certification body pursuant to Article 43, authorize contractual clauses and provisions referred to in Article 46(3), approve binding corporate rules pursuant to Article 47, contribute to the activities of the Board, keep internal records of infringements of this Regulation and of measures taken in accordance with Article 58(2), and fulfill any other tasks related to the protection of personal data.

Their primary responsibilities include the monitoring and enforcement of the Regulation, promoting public awareness (especially for children), and advising governmental bodies on legislative measures. They act as a resource for data subjects by providing information on rights and handling complaints. 

Operationally, they must cooperate with other supervisory authorities to ensure consistency in enforcement and conduct investigations into the application of the Regulation. Their technical tasks include establishing lists for data protection impact assessments, providing advice on specific processing operations, and adopting standard contractual clauses. 

Furthermore, they play a role in quality assurance by encouraging and approving codes of conduct, certification mechanisms, and binding corporate rules, as well as drafting criteria and conducting accreditation for monitoring and certification bodies. They are also required to maintain internal records of infringements and measures taken. 

Regarding costs, the performance of these tasks shall be free of charge for the data subject and, where applicable, the data protection officer. question: When shall the Board issue an opinion and what are the measures that trigger this obligation?
gold_answer: The Board shall issue an opinion when a competent supervisory authority intends to adopt any of the measures listed in Article 64(1), which are: (a) adopting a list of processing operations subject to a DPIA; (b) assessing whether a draft code of conduct complies with the GDPR; (c) approving the criteria for accreditation of a body or certification body; (d) determining standard data protection clauses or authorizing contractual clauses; or (e) approving binding corporate rules.
llm_response:

1. When a competent supervisory authority intends to adopt any of the measures specified in Article 64(1) and communicates the draft decision to the Board. 
2. When any supervisory authority, the Chair of the Board, or the Commission requests the examination of a matter of general application or one producing effects in more than one Member State (Article 64(2)).
3. When a supervisory authority requests an urgent opinion due to an urgent need to act to protect the rights and freedoms of data subjects (Article 66(3)).
4. When a supervisory authority has taken a provisional measure and considers that final measures need urgently to be adopted (Article 66(2)).

Provided the Board has not already issued an opinion on the same matter, it shall adopt the opinion within eight weeks, extendable by six weeks (Article 64(3)).', 'full_answer': "The Board's obligation to issue an opinion is triggered by several specific measures and requests. Under Article 64(1), the Board shall issue an opinion when a competent supervisory authority intends to adopt certain measures and communicates the draft decision to the Board. In urgent scenarios, Article 66 provides two triggers: first, where a supervisory authority has already adopted a provisional measure and deems final measures urgently necessary (Article 66(2)); second, where a supervisory authority requests an urgent opinion because another competent authority has failed to take appropriate measures in a situation requiring urgent action to protect data subjects' rights and freedoms (Article 66(3)).

Regarding timing, Article 64(3) stipulates that the Board shall issue the opinion within eight weeks, provided no prior opinion on the same matter exists. This period may be extended by an additional six weeks depending on the complexity of the subject. question: What is the purpose of the implementing acts that may be adopted by the Commission in accordance with Article 67?
gold_answer: The purpose of the implementing acts is to specify the arrangements for the exchange of information by electronic means between supervisory authorities, and between supervisory authorities and the Board, in particular the standardised format referred to in Article 64.
llm_response: {'answer': 'The purpose of the implementing acts that the Commission may adopt in accordance with Article 67 is to specify the arrangements for the exchange of information by electronic means between supervisory authorities, as well as between supervisory authorities and the Board, with a particular focus on the standardised format referred to in Article 64.', 'full_answer': 'Pursuant to Article 67, the Commission is empowered to adopt implementing acts of general scope. The specific function of these acts is to define and specify the arrangements necessary for the exchange of information via electronic means. This exchange occurs between two primary sets of actors: between different supervisory authorities and between supervisory authorities and the European Data Protection Board. Furthermore, these implementing acts are specifically intended to address the standardised format for such exchanges as mentioned in Article 64. According to the provided text, these implementing acts must be adopted following the examination procedure set out in Article 93(2).', 'key_snippet': 'specify the arrangements for the exchange of information by electronic means between supervisory authorities', 'chunks': ['chapter_vii_article_67'], 'percentage': ['100']}

question: What is the required majority for the Board to adopt its own rules of procedure?
gold_answer: A two-thirds majority of its members.
llm_response: {'answer': 'The Board shall adopt its own rules of procedure by a two-thirds majority of its members, as established in Article 72, paragraph 2.', 'full_answer': 'According to the provided legal text, specifically Article 72, paragraph 2, the European Data Protection Board is required to adopt its own rules of procedure through a two-thirds majority of its members. This specific majority requirement is an exception to the general rule established in Article 72, paragraph 1, which states that the Board typically takes decisions by a simple majority of its members unless otherwise provided for in the Regulation. Therefore, for the specific purpose of adopting rules of procedure and organizing its own operational arrangements, the higher threshold of a two-thirds majority is mandatory.', 'key_snippet': 'The Board shall adopt its own rules of procedure by a two-thirds majority of its members', 'chunks': ['chapter_vii_article_72'], 'percentage': ['100']}

question: What is the purpose of the specific rules that Member States may adopt in relation to controllers or processors subject to an obligation of secrecy under Union or Member State law?
gold_answer: The purpose of the specific rules is to reconcile the right of the protection of personal data with the obligation of secrecy.
llm_response: {'answer': 'Member States may adopt specific rules to set out the powers of supervisory authorities (specifically those in points (e) and (f) of Article 58(1)) regarding controllers or processors subject to an obligation of professional secrecy or equivalent obligations under Union or Member State law. The purpose of these rules is to reconcile the right to the protection of personal data with the obligation of secrecy, provided such measures are necessary and proportionate. These rules apply exclusively to personal data received or obtained during an activity covered by said obligation of secrecy (Chapter IX, Article 90, point 1).', 'full_answer': 'According to Chapter IX, Article 90, point 1, Member States are permitted to establish specific rules concerning the powers of supervisory authorities—specifically those outlined in points (e) and (f) of Article 58(1)—when dealing with controllers or processors who are bound by professional secrecy or equivalent obligations under Union or Member State law, or rules from national competent bodies. The objective of these specific rules is to achieve a balance between the fundamental right to the protection of personal data and the legal obligation of secrecy, ensuring that the measures adopted are both necessary and proportionate. Furthermore, the scope of these rules is limited: they apply only to personal data that the controller or processor acquired as a result of, or obtained during, the specific activity that is subject to the obligation of secrecy.', 'key_snippet': 'where this is necessary and proportionate to reconcile the right of the protection of personal data with the obligation of secrecy', 'chunks': ['chapter IX, article 90, point 1'], 'percentage': ['100']}

question: What information must the controller provide to the data subject at the time when personal data are obtained, according to Article 13?
gold_answer: The controller must provide the data subject with the identity and contact details of the controller and, where applicable, of the controller's representative; the contact details of the data protection officer, where applicable; the purposes of the processing for which the personal data are intended as well as the legal basis for the processing; where the processing is based on point (f) of Article 6(1), the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party; the recipients or categories of recipients of the personal data, if any; where applicable, the fact that the controller intends to transfer personal data to a third country or international organisation and the existence or absence of an adequacy decision by the Commission, or in the case of transfers referred to in Article 46 or 47, or the second subparagraph of Article 49(1), reference to the appropriate or suitable safeguards and the means by which to obtain a copy of them or where they have been made available. question: When is a controller or processor not established in the Union exempt from designating a representative in the Union under Article 27?
gold_answer: The obligation laid down in paragraph 1 of this Article shall not apply to: (a) processing which is occasional, does not include, on a large scale, processing of special categories of data as referred to in Article 9(1) or processing of personal data relating to criminal convictions and offences referred to in Article 10, and is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons, taking into account the nature, context, scope and purposes of the processing; or (b) a public authority or body.

However, Article 27(2) establishes two specific exemptions from this obligation. First, the exemption applies to processing that meets three cumulative criteria: it must be occasional, it must not involve the large-scale processing of special categories of data as defined in Article 9(1) or data relating to criminal convictions and offences as defined in Article 10, and it must be unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons, taking into account the nature, context, scope, and purposes of the processing. Second, the obligation to designate a representative does not apply to any public authority or body. question: What are the tasks of each supervisory authority according to Article 57 of the GDPR Regulation?
gold_answer: Each supervisory authority shall monitor and enforce the application of the GDPR Regulation, promote public awareness and understanding of the risks, rules, safeguards, and rights in relation to processing, advise the national parliament, the government, and other institutions and bodies on legislative and administrative measures relating to the protection of natural persons' rights and freedoms with regard to processing, promote the awareness of controllers and processors of their obligations under this Regulation, provide information to any data subject concerning the exercise of their rights under this Regulation and, if appropriate, cooperate with the supervisory authorities in other Member States to that end, handle complaints lodged by a data subject, or by a body, organization, or association in accordance with Article 80, and investigate, to the extent appropriate, the subject matter of the complaint and inform the complainant of the progress and the outcome of the investigation within a reasonable period, in particular if further investigation or coordination with another supervisory authority is necessary, cooperate with, including sharing information and provide mutual assistance to, other supervisory authorities with a view to ensuring the consistency of application and enforcement of this Regulation, conduct investigations on the application of this Regulation, including on the basis of information received from another supervisory authority or other public authority, monitor relevant developments, insofar as they have an impact on the protection of personal data, in particular the development of information and communication technologies and commercial practices, adopt standard contractual clauses referred to in Article 28(8) and in point (d) of Article 46(2), establish and maintain a list in relation to the requirement for data protection impact assessment pursuant to Article 35(4), give advice on the processing operations referred to in Article 36(2), encourage the drawing up of codes of conduct pursuant to Article 40(1) and provide an opinion and approve such codes of conduct which provide sufficient safeguards, pursuant to Article 40(5), encourage the establishment of data protection certification mechanisms and of data protection seals and marks pursuant to Article 42(1), and approve the criteria of certification pursuant to Article 42(5), where applicable, carry out a periodic review of certifications issued in accordance with Article 42(7), draft and publish the criteria for accreditation of a body for monitoring codes of conduct pursuant to Article 41 and of a certification body pursuant to Article 43, conduct the accreditation of a body for monitoring codes of conduct pursuant to Article 41 and of a certification body pursuant to Article 43, authorize contractual clauses and provisions referred to in Article 46(3), approve binding corporate rules pursuant to Article 47, contribute to the activities of the Board, keep internal records of infringements of this Regulation and of measures taken in accordance with Article 58(2), and fulfill any other tasks related to the protection of personal data.

question: Under what circumstances can Union or Member State law restrict the scope of the obligations and rights provided for in Articles 12 to 22 and Article 34, as well as Article 5?
gold_answer: Union or Member State law may restrict the scope of the obligations and rights provided for in Articles 12 to 22 and Article 34, as well as Article 5, when such a restriction respects the essence of the fundamental rights and freedoms and is a necessary and proportionate measure in a democratic society to safeguard national security, defence, public security, the prevention, investigation, detection or prosecution of criminal offences or the execution of criminal penalties, including the safeguarding against and the prevention of threats to public security, other important objectives of general public interest of the Union or of a Member State, in particular an important economic or financial interest of the Union or of a Member State, including monetary, budgetary and taxation a matters, public health and social security, the protection of judicial independence and judicial proceedings, the prevention, investigation, detection and prosecution of breaches of ethics for regulated professions, a monitoring, inspection or regulatory function connected, even occasionally, to the exercise of official authority in the cases referred to in points (a) to (e) and (g), the protection of the data subject or the rights and freedoms of others, or the enforcement of civil law claims.

This is permissible provided that the provisions correspond to the rights and obligations in Articles 12 to 22, and the restriction respects the essence of the fundamental rights and freedoms. Additionally, the restriction must be a necessary and proportionate measure in a democratic society to safeguard specific interests (Chapter III, Article 23, Point 1).', 'full_answer': 'According to Chapter III, Article 23, Point 1, the legal framework of the Union or a Member State may impose restrictions on the scope of rights and obligations established in Articles 12 to 22, Article 34, and Article 5. For such a restriction to be legally valid, it must meet the following cumulative conditions: 1) It must be implemented by way of a legislative measure; 2) The provisions of that law must correspond to the rights and obligations provided for in Articles 12 to 22; 3) The restriction must respect the essence of the fundamental rights and freedoms; and 4) The measure must be both necessary and proportionate within a democratic society to safeguard the relevant interests. question: What information must the controller provide to the data subject at the time when personal data are obtained, according to Article 13?
gold_answer: The controller must provide the data subject with the identity and contact details of the controller and, where applicable, of the controller's representative; the contact details of the data protection officer, where applicable; the purposes of the processing for which the personal data are intended as well as the legal basis for the processing; where the processing is based on point (f) of Article 6(1), the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party; the recipients or categories of recipients of the personal data, if any; where applicable, the fact that the controller intends to transfer personal data to a third country or international organisation and the existence or absence of an adequacy decision by the Commission, or in the case of transfers referred to in Article 46 or 47, or the second subparagraph of Article 49(1), reference to the appropriate or suitable safeguards and the means by which to obtain a copy of them or where they have been made available. In addition, the controller must provide the data subject with the period for which the personal data will be stored, or if that is not possible, the criteria used to determine that period; the existence of the right to request from the controller access to and rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing concerning the data subject or to object to processing as well as the right to data portability; where the processing is based on point (a) of Article 6(1) or point (a) of Article 9(2), the existence of the right to withdraw consent at any time, without affecting the lawfulness of processing based on consent before its withdrawal; the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority; whether the provision of personal data is a statutory or contractual requirement, or a requirement necessary to enter into a contract, as well as whether the data subject is obliged to provide the personal data and of the possible consequences of failure to provide such data; the existence of automated decision-making, including profiling, referred to in Article 22(1) and (4) and, at least in those cases, meaningful information about the logic involved, as well as the significance and the envisaged consequences of such processing for the data subject.

question: What information must the controller provide to the data subject at the time when personal data are obtained, according to Article 13?
gold_answer: The controller must provide the data subject with the identity and contact details of the controller and, where applicable, of the controller's representative; the contact details of the data protection officer, where applicable; the purposes of the processing for which the personal data are intended as well as the legal basis for the processing; where the processing is based on point (f) of Article 6(1), the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party; the recipients or categories of recipients of the personal data, if any; where applicable, the fact that the controller intends to transfer personal data to a third country or international organisation and the existence or absence of an adequacy decision by the Commission, or in the case of transfers referred to in Article 46 or 47, or the second subparagraph of Article 49(1), reference to the appropriate or suitable safeguards and the means by which to obtain a copy of them or where they have been made available. In addition, the controller must provide the data subject with the period for which the personal data will be stored, or if that is not possible, the criteria used to determine that period; the existence of the right to request from the controller access to and rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing concerning the data subject or to object to processing as well as the right to data portability; where the processing is based on point (a) of Article 6(1) or point (a) of Article 9(2), the existence of the right to withdraw consent at any time, without affecting the lawfulness of processing based on consent before its withdrawal; the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority; whether the provision of personal data is a statutory or contractual requirement, or a requirement necessary to enter into a contract, as well as whether the data subject is obliged to provide the personal data and of the possible consequences of failure to provide such data; the existence of automated decision-making, including profiling, referred to in Article 22(1) and (4) and, at least in those cases, meaningful information about the logic involved, as well as the significance and the envisaged consequences of such processing for the data subject.
